title: Always Late
description:
‘I wanted to be unreliable. I liked being trapped in the present. This was what lateness allowed – invisibility, being nowhere.’ 

Anna Metcalfe on postponement.

The post Always Late appeared first on Granta.
hook: She spent years being late on purpose—it was the only way to stay invisible and live entirely in the present moment.

title: The Uncomfortable Lightness of Dark Academia
description:
“Asked where she got the idea for the mystery novel, the author said she was inspired by a colleague in her real academic life who does ‘noble’ work but is ‘such an asshole.’”

The post The Uncomfortable Lightness of Dark Academia appeared first on Public Books.
hook: Why dark academia keeps recycling the same smug assholes: a genre built on moral superiority with nothing to say.

title: Lady C by Guy Cuthbertson review – how Lady Chatterley’s Lover rocked Britain
description:
A history of the social and cultural impact of DH Lawrence’s novel shows how it inspired comedy as well as controversy

Not known for his humour, DH Lawrence thought of Lady Chatterley’s Lover as a serious novel about the sacred nature of sex. But some of the activity between Connie and the gamekeeper Mellors is funny, either unintentionally (as in the scene where they garland each other’s naked bodies with flowers) or with a playful recognition of carnal absurdity: his penis is “fa
hook: DH Lawrence wrote Lady Chatterley as sacred sex. The British turned it into comedy. A book's meaning belongs to its readers.

title: A Novel That Refuses the Korean War’s Erasure
description:
Eve J. Chung’s sophomore novel, The Young Will Remember, turns its gaze to a lesser-known corner of twentieth-century history—the Korean War and its aftershocks. At its center is Ellie, an American journalist whose plane crashes in enemy territory. She’s rescued by Emma, a North Korean woman searching for her daughter who was taken years earlier […]

The post A Novel That Refuses the Korean War&
hook: A plane crash in North Korea becomes the frame for two women's colliding histories—a war the world forgot, told through intimate urgency.

title: Pass This On: Olof Dreijer’s Favourite Records
description:
Ahead of the release of his debut solo LP, Loud Bloom, the Swedish producer and DJ talks Gemma Samways through the albums that have shaped his inimitable sound, from Missy Elliott to Oumou Sangaré

Photo by Bo Bannink

Olof Dreijer can’t remember a time when politics weren’t intrinsic to his outlook. Raised in Gothenburg
hook: What shaped Olof Dreijer before his solo debut—the records that built the artist, chosen by him, explained by him.
